title: Cat Sudden Loss of Coordination: Emergency Guide & Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your cat stumbling or losing balance? Sudden loss of coordination is a high-priority emergency. Learn the causes and why you must act fast.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden loss of coordination in cats is a medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary attention to address potential neurological issues, toxins, or infections. Capturing a short video of your cat’s behavior before heading to the clinic can significantly assist your veterinarian in providing a fast and accurate diagnosis.

What does it mean if my cat suddenly loses coordination?

Sudden loss of coordination, often referred to as ataxia, occurs when your cat loses the ability to control their body movements normally. You may notice your cat stumbling, swaying, falling over, or appearing 'drunk' while walking. This condition is usually caused by a disruption in the nervous system, the inner ear (vestibular system), or the brain, affecting their sense of space and balance.

Is my cat's sudden loss of balance a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. The sudden onset of balance issues is a medical emergency that requires immediate evaluation.
  • Symptoms can be caused by serious underlying conditions such as strokes, neurological trauma, toxin ingestion, or severe infections.
  • Prompt intervention is critical to ensure the best possible outcome for your pet.

How can taking a video of my cat's behavior help the veterinarian?

  • Capturing a short video of your cat's behavior is an invaluable tool for the veterinary team, especially since cats often hide symptoms during travel.
  • A video allows the vet to see exactly how your cat was moving at home in a stress-free environment.
  • Footage helps the vet distinguish between types of ataxia or identify involuntary eye movements (nystagmus) that may have subsided by the time you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with sudden loss of coordination, it is critical to differentiate the lesion location through neurological examination. Cerebellar lesions typically manifest as ataxia and dysmetria, while vestibular dysfunction causes ipsilateral falling, rolling, or circling. If no head abnormalities are evident, spinal cord, peripheral nerve, or muscle lesions are more likely. Examination of the neck, including assessment for pain and atrophy, along with postural reaction tests like wheelbarrowing and conscious proprioceptive positioning, can aid in detecting subtle deficits of the thoracic limbs and cervical spinal cord. Feline nonsuppurative meningoencephalomyelitis, a slowly progressive inflammatory CNS disease, should also be considered, although the cause is unknown.

Chapter: Neurology, Parasitology, Infectious Diseases

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1252)

faqs.question: Can an ear infection cause my cat to lose its balance?
faqs.answer: Yes, a deep inner ear infection can affect the vestibular system, which is responsible for balance, leading to stumbling and a head tilt.

faqs.question: Is it possible my cat had a stroke?
faqs.answer: While less common in cats than in humans, strokes or vascular events can occur and often manifest as a sudden loss of coordination or weakness on one side of the body.

faqs.question: How should I transport my cat to the emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Place your cat in a secure, well-padded carrier to prevent them from falling or injuring themselves further during the car ride.
